WIOL Season and Winter O' Series To Begin

Don't forget about the 1995-96 Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League season, which officially begins with the January 6 Ft. Steilacoom Park meet in Tacoma. Support the league by helping at a meet or bringing a student to a meet.

The first meet of the Winter O' Series will also be at Ft. Steilacoom. No pre-registration is required to participate in the Series. Just run the longest course at a minimum of 3 of the 5 designated Series meets. You will receive a score based on your placing at each meet you attend. Your best three scores will be totaled and male and female winners will be crowned at the final meet at Seward Park on February 24. The full Winter O' Series schedule follows:

January
6 – Ft. Steilacoom Park, Tacoma
14 – Marymoor Park, Redmond
27 – Carkeek Park, Seattle

February
10 – Bridle Trails Park, Kirkland
24 – Seward Park, Seattle

For more information about WIOL or the O' Series, contact Debbie Newell at 206-488-7471 or DebbieN@cuix.pscu.com.

COC's Life Service Award Recipients: An Interview

via electronic mail, by Rick Hood

This year's COC Life Service Award Recipients collectively have over 30 years of volunteer service in support of our sport. Accordingly, we thought you might like to hear from honorees Debbie Newell and Harvey Friedman in their own words. (The COC board also chose to recognize Mike Schuh for his 15 plus years of service with this award, but we just learned he declined. However, in the spirit of community and growing the sport strong, we have included his responses as well.) — Rick

Congratulations on your COC life service award recognition.

[Debbie] Thank you. It's always nice to be appreciated, even when you do something of your own choice which you enjoy.

[Harvey] Thank you. I am grateful for the honor. Might I suggest renaming it the 15 Year Service Award and try to see who qualifies for 5-year service awards at each annual meeting banquet/party. It might keep more members who can not 'win' medals in the woods interested longer. Many folks have dropped out of O' locally and not just those who have moved out of the area or who have had children. Anything to try to keep more people involved should be tried.

[Mike] As I describe elsewhere, I have declined the award.

Which of your many contributions over the years to COC are you most proud of and why?

[Debbie] My stint as newsletter editor was the most enjoyable, but also the most work. It's a key position in the club. The newsletter lets all our members know the club is alive and kicking.

[Harvey] I got Cascade O. C. 501(c)(3) status with the IRS in 1983-84, was able to maintain a viable club as president during the difficult period when

Page 2

two new offshoot clubs formed, was event organizer/meet director for the 1990 PNWOF World Cup event which until 1993 WOC was the meet with the largest individual entrant attendance in the USA, was newsletter editor for 2 years, was membership coordinator for 8 years. Surprisingly enough I am still willing to do occasional course design and more often to work the registration table at local meets.

[Mike] Tough question. What am I most proud of? The fact that I have been a member of COC longer than just about anyone? That I probably have contributed to more maps, more events, and the development of more clubs than any other member? That I have introduced more people to orienteering than anyone else in the region? I don't think I can pick any specific contributions—in all immodesty, there are just too many to choose from. I think the activities that have made me feel the best about my involvement in orienteering are those where I have made someone else happy by introducing them to this wonderful sport. To toil long and hard creating a map (often in the rain), then set a course (also in the rain), and then help some first timers find their way around the course, and to have them come back to the finish, all excited and eager to try another course—this is much, much more rewarding to me than any "prestigious" award, and in fact such awards pale in comparison.

What path would you suggest to those who want to get more involved with helping COC evolve, and what direction do you think the club might wish to aim toward?

[Debbie] I think our club must aim toward getting more kids involved in the sport. We must also educate the general public about orienteering so they will realize that it is not destructive to the environment. All it takes to get involved with the club is a call to a board member or any other active member of the club.

[Harvey] To quote the Nike ad, 'just do it'. If one hasn't helped out before, is at a meet and thinks that now would be a good time to start, it is easy. After you finish your course(s), ask the meet director if you are needed to help pick up the markers. This is a good way to see where placement is on a course you might not have tried and will be welcomed by the tired course setter and meet director, both of whom had to rise early to provide such a successful event. With enough people helping, everyone is back in enough time to all go get refreshments together at a local (somewhat close) restaurant. If you ask, you will probably learn what is involved in putting on a local meet.

So, volunteer to be a timer at the next meet. The meet director will try to have enough people so that there can be early and late shifts so that every one gets a chance to do a course. Over 4 meets, do start timing, finish timing, results posting, registration. Then you will know how to handle the administrative side of a local meet and you will be ready to be a meet director yourself. By working closely with the course designer, you will learn something about courses and, after taking a class in course design, you will be ready to do your own courses in a local park with the help of a course consultant.

Thus, when there is stretch of 2 or 3 weekends when the usual club organizers all travel to out-of-state meets and you think there should be a local meet during that time, you will be experienced enough to organize and run a successful local meet for those not traveling and any newcomers you might entice. Such satisfaction.

[Mike] The first part of the question has an easy answer: attend the club's board meetings, as that is where decisions are made regarding the present and future of the club. For those members unable to attend a meeting, offer to help out at an event, either by calling the Meet Director ahead of time (preferred!), or by volunteering at the event to help pick up markers or clean up.

The second part has two answers: First, I really have no idea where the club "might wish to aim towards". Part of my criticisms of last Spring (which led to my resigning from the board) were on this precise point: the club has no stated set of goals. The second answer would be my thoughts on where I would prefer the club to head. As this is a long answer, I have written a separate article elsewhere in this newsletter.

In spite of, or maybe because of, your years of service, I know you still enjoy being out on a course. What makes orienteering enjoyable for you both locally and elsewhere, and what would you suggest in helping others discover and experience the same?

[Debbie] I enjoy orienteering because every meet is a different experience, with new challenges to meet. And I like traveling to other areas, meeting new people and seeing old friends. Orienteers are generally a wonderful group of people, no matter where you are in the world.

[Harvey] Because I still make so many errors due to lapses in concentration, I still am in search of the perfect run. I don't expect that will happen but nevertheless I get a feeling of satisfaction every time I choose a good route and the control marker is right there. I have a feeling of relief when I finally find it after whatever errors I might have made. I

Page 3

try to design courses so that people will enjoy the sport as much as I do.

One gets both physical and mental exercise and gets to meet many interesting people. Being open to hosting out-of-area orienteers in your house or apartment lets you learn from others about places you might not have been before. When you can afford to, you can then travel to other areas and have reciprocal lodging provided as well. Important for me is that most orienteers do not smoke.

Of course, if you go to a small town in a foreign country where the non-orienteering locals don't travel much, you may be up talking way past mid-nite even though you have an early start the next day. Americans (not the obnoxious youngsters nor the groups of retirees) are objects of interest to many in the civilized world. It is much fun.

[Mike] Orienteering combines an intellectual challenge—problem solving—with (usually) outdoor exercise; since I enjoy both endeavors, I enjoy a sport that has both. Because orienteers do not physically compete against each other (as in boxing), the atmosphere around even the most competitive of events is a friendly one. My involvement in orienteering has led me to travel to places that I might never have visited otherwise, and as a consequence I have met a lot of interesting friends and have orienteered in a lot of different terrain (10 foreign countries, and more than half of the states).

I would encourage others to invite their friends and family to our local events, and to walk around a couple of courses with them. An individual's first experience with orienteering can determine whether they return for another event; it is very important, therefore, that everyone associated with an event do their very best to make that first experience an enjoyable one—at every event. The map should be accurate and legible, and the courses should be appropriate with the markers correctly placed. Meet registration should be simple and smooth, and results processing should be prompt and correct. In general, be friendly, be helpful, be enthusiastic, and, most of all, look like you're having fun.

Any closing comments?

[Debbie] Thank you again for the award. Orienteering is great sport because you can participate in so many different ways. You can be competitive or you can simply enjoy being out in the woods. You can become involved in club administration or you can be out in the field setting courses and mapping. No matter which way you go you can't make a bad route choice.

[Harvey] Orienteering is the greatest sport in the world. Anyone can participate as long as they can walk, see, and think. My personal long-term goal is to compete in the Swedish five-day in the men's 100+ year old class. There currently is a 90+ class with both men and women actively out in the woods.

Orienteering is a great family sport. All can do a course together to start and as the relative abilities separate people, they can do courses separately. Children who go out alone learn responsible decision making. There is no intimidation factor as in such sports as football and basketball that might discourage kids from playing. One of the reasons for the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (WIOL) is to help kids develop their self-confidence (not just to get more Cascade members, though that is nice, too). Invite a friend to try our fabulous sport.

[Mike] I feel it is unfortunate that COC's leadership has chosen to hand out "Life Service Awards" in this fashion. It appears to me that the award was created as a direct consequence of my resignation from the COC Executive Board last Spring, and this makes the award feel a bit artificial to me. Instead of acting on some of my grievances, the board decided to give me an award. Also, last year a "President's Service Award" was presented to Peter Golde. Why was there no such award this year? There does not seem to an established recognition and award program within the club.

My contributions to local orienteering were never designed to win me any award. If they had any coherent design (doubtful in some cases), it was to further the sport, to get more people involved in it. My rewards have always come from the joy I bring to the participants; a "prestigious" Life Service Award does little to improve upon this, and in fact seems superfluous.

Accepting the award will not improve orienteering in the region, nor will it introduce more people to the sport, nor will it make any new maps, and it certainly won't set any courses. Only action does these things. Instead of acting, however, the board invented an award. I do not wish to receive the award, and I feel that being forced to make this decision has in turn caused some ill will: for this I am sorry. I hope that we can put all of this fluff behind us and move forward. Thank you.

Again, on behalf of the club and the greater orienteering community, thank you all for your comments and congratulations Debbie and Harvey on your award; we are very proud of you.

From the Tales of Sacajawea

Elis Eberlein, President, SACO

SACO had its annual meeting on December 2. It had good showing of local participant and was followed by the traditional Swedish Christmas Smorgasbord. At the meeting the following Board members were chosen for one year terms: Elis Eberlein, President; Frank Skorina, Ruth Miller, Michel Billaux, and Bob Gruel.

The new thing for 1996 is that the meet program will contain several meet on Sunday afternoons rather than Saturday mornings. This will be tested to decrease the conflicts with other youth sports activities. SACO will also organize a two-day event in Walla Walla and Horn Rapids in April. This is event arranged to make it more feasible for travelers from other areas to visit our area and try our maps. This will be arranged on April 27-28.

Another discussion at the annual meeting was regarding organizing a major meet in our part of the state in cooperation with EWOC in Spokane. The discussion is to try to organize the Intercollegiate Championships 1997 which are traditionally held during the month of April. A possible area for such an event would be the area just south of Potholes Reservoir, south of Moses Lake. A decision about this has to be made in early 1996 to make the mapping project and organization possible. All possible help and input will be appreciated. I will probably write about this further in the next issue of Bearing.

Upcoming Ski-O

There is a planned Ski-Orienteering event this winter at Lake Wenatchee on Sunday, March 3.

Ski Orienteering is a mixture of foot orienteering and cross country skiing. Taking place largely on groomed trails, ski orienteering challenges map reading, route finding and skiing techniques. For various reasons, ski- orienteering has been sort of left behind in the Northwest, so set aside March 3 to experience the challenge and fun of a day in the snow.

Clinic
For those who want or need a little guidance or refresher in cross country technique, there will be a clinic Saturday, March 2 at Bruce McAlister's cabin near Scenic. The clinic will be from 10 to 2. Following the clinic, some hardy souls will ski or snowshoe into the Scenic Hot Springs. Those who will be continuing on to Lake Wenatchee are welcome to stay overnight at the cabin. It is about a 1 k walk or ski in to the rustic cabin: no electricity, no running water, but lots of atmosphere. Bring a sleeping bag. Dinner and breakfast is $10.

Ski-O Meet
The meet will be on groomed trails at Lake Wenatchee State Park. There will be a B/W map. Starts are from 10 to 12 at the skiing parking lot (area will be signed).

Directions
For the clinic proceed over the Stevens Pass Highway (US 2) to the chain-up area at Scenic, about 2 miles beyond Deception Falls. Proceed to the far end of the chain-up area and park in plowed area with cabins in the background. This is NOT a Sno-Park area. Take the old Stevens Pass road across from parking area about 1 k to cabin (signed). Approximately 60 miles from Seattle.

For the meet continue about 5 miles on US 2 to Stevens Pass and continue about 19 miles to the Lake Wenatchee turn-off. The turn-off from US 2 is well signed. The park itself is about 3 miles north of US 2, and is well marked. Proceed to the cross country ski-area parking lot. The start area will be signed from here. This parking area IS a sno-park area, and your vehicle must have a permit.

Costs
Meet Costs: $5/person; $2 for juniors under 18. (Dinner and breakfast optional Saturday night - $10). Please note this meet is sponsored by the the US Ski Orienteering Team, and is not sponsored by any local club. There are NO club or USOF discounts. All proceeds over expenses go to the US Ski Orienteering Team.

For information and reservations for dinner and overnight call Bruce McAlister (206) 821-6862.
Please remember to check with the hot-line as all Ski events are subject to weather and cancellation or relocation.

A Vision for Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest

Mike Schuh

Last Spring I asked the Executive Board of Cascade OC "what are the club's goals? where are we headed?" Not too surprisingly, no one had an answer—COC had no stated goals, nor any kind of long term plan. We seem to proceed in fits and starts from one event to the next, from one year to another. Often, club events come upon us before we are ready for them, and what little advance planning that is done takes on the air of last minute crisis management. I don't think it has to be this way.

I would like to propose a set of long term goals for orienteering in the Pacific Northwest. Some of these might seem outlandish, or even impossible, while others would appear to be obvious. In the absence of any kind of long range discussion elsewhere, let me present a few possible goals, and then suggest how we might reach them. For each goal, I ask that we first agree that it is a worthy goal, and then figure how we can make it happen. Too often these discussions run into attitudes like "we can't do that" (which almost guarantees that we can't!) and "who'd want to do that?" Please, let's separate the "what" (the goal) from the "how". And remember that these are long term goals...

Develop more small clubs, and help existing clubs survive and grow.
Specifically, develop clubs in the following Washington cities: Bremerton/Poulsbo, Port Townsend, Port Angeles, Hoquiam/Aberdeen, Centralia/Chehalis, Yakima, Wenatchee, Omak/Okanogan, Moses Lake, and Pullman/Moscow (ID). In Oregon: Eugene, Albany/Corvalis, Salem, Bend/Redmond, The Dalles, Astoria, Roseburg, Klamath Falls, Pendleton, and Baker. Idaho: Boise, Pocatello, Idaho Falls, Twin Falls, Sandpoint, and Coeur d' Alene. Montana is tougher, but start with the bigger cities: Missoula, Butte, Helena, Great Falls, and Billings. Some of these could be combined to start with - Sandpoint and Coeur d' Alene will probably operate as a "chapter" of the EWOC in the short term, and initially there would be just one club on the West side of Puget Sound.

I firmly believe that a rising tide of orienteering will lift all clubs. That is, having a club just down the road from another club will help both clubs grow and prosper. Here in Seattle we see this symbiosis with the high degree of cooperation between COC, SAMM, and HUSKY. In Eastern Washington, despite the distance involved, EWOC and SACO are working together on various events and projects. As we form more clubs, this just gets better. If we had nearly twenty clubs in Washington State, then the attendance at major events would increase and hosting them would be less of a financial risk. In addition, the registration fees from such a large orienteering population would provide the funds necessary to produce more maps. In addition, I feel that a larger orienteering populace will help us lobby for land access.

In other words, the promotion and growth of orienteering are much more important than "prestigious" awards, ranking systems, making O' an Olympic sport, earning a World Championships title, or just about anything else. If the sport grows, then these other goals become easier; conversely, these other goals do little by themselves to expand orienteering.

And, besides, it'll just be a lot more fun.

We also need to take care of what we have. Several clubs are just barely hanging on, in particular JSOC in Medford and CHUKO in Anacortes. We need to help these clubs become self-sufficient and stable.

Host at least six two day Class A meets each year.
Might as well suggest an "outlandish" one. The presumption here is that Class A meets are worth hosting and attending. For those who care about national ranking points, this is arguably the best way to earn them. For the rest of us, a Class A meet means (or is supposed to mean) top notch organization with good courses on a first rate map. I feel they are worth doing, and I think that we could host more than we presently do. COC could host one a year (we even agreed to do this a year or two ago, but...) while SAMM, NISQ, CROC, and EWOC/SACO/EOC could host one every other year. This would give us three such events each year. As other clubs develop and grow, they could host additional events (this is why goal #1 is #1).

Sure this is a long term goal, but think what it means: instead of using up vacation time and spending big bucks to fly all around the country, we could DRIVE to Class A meets. Wouldn't that be nice?

Make at least one of these Class A meets an IOF event.
Why not? Mostly it's a bit of extra paper work, but it would give us some wider exposure, and perhaps a larger attendance.

Host at least one national championship each year.
Similar to #3. COC has discussed hosting the Intercollegiate Championships as well as the US

Page 6

Championships; we hosted the Short Course Championships in 1994.

Expand WIOL beyond the greater Seattle area.
Well, first we need to focus on increasing the level of participation within the present WIOL area, but expansion throughout Western Washington should be feasible and rewarding. During the 1994-95 season, the league essentially was a one-school league (Bremerton HS students were about 70-80% of the attendance at most of the meets). The long term health of the league depends on expanding it to other schools. Climatic concerns make expansion in Eastern Washington a bit problematic - the present Winter season won't work over there.
Expansion anywhere, however, is dependent on clubs and maps existing in those new areas. Hence goal #1.
We could also explore adoption of orienteering by WIAA (the state high school athletics association). At the moment, I think WIOL is too small, but we could investigate what it requires and what it would mean. The benefit is that schools could issue varsity letters to participants, which would mean more exposure for the sport.

Host the USOF convention at least once every five to ten years.
Our best chance to influence national O' policy, and to gain the right to host national events. Plus, it's a money maker and brings national expertise to our area.

Have a Class A map within an hour's drive of 90% of Washington's population by 1998.
Actually, we're almost there now. Trollhaugen, Drunken Charlie Lake, and Tenalquot are each within an hour's drive of most of the Seattle/Tacoma/Olympia area (75% of the state's population), and Riverside sits on the edge of Spokane. If we upgrade and expand Fire Mtn. or make a map near North Bend (or Bremerton), and CROC maps Camp Bonneville, then we'd probably have it.
But why bother with such a goal? Because this would mean that orienteering is accessible to just about everyone in the state. The farther away the event (regardless of its nature), the greater the resistance for first timers to go and try it.
This goal could be reworded as "have an orienteering map (of any size) within a 30 minute drive of 100% of Washington's population by 2000". This might be more useful. It will also be more work... (45 minutes, 98%, and 2005 might be more realistic - but the point is to have a goal).

For WSOA: Publish a guide book to regional orienteering maps.
Something simple like how to get to each map in the region. Nothing fancy, and it could be a fund raiser.

For COC: Host at least 24 days of orienteering each year, and work to increase the attendance at each event.
Events are orienteering clubs' raison d'etre—they are how we teach the sport, and they are our largest source of income. Yes, they take work to organize and host, but it is foolish to consciously hold fewer of them, as is often proposed. Yes, there are other clubs in the immediate area that host events, but COC usually does not earn any income from those events.
I strongly believe that both the club and the sport benefit from having an active schedule. If, at some future date, our events average 200 or more in attendance, then we won't need as many of them to generate revenue. COC could then reduce our event schedule, but this would make sense only when there are other clubs able to fill the void (see goal #1!).
In 1995, there was an orienteering event somewhere in Washington State nearly every weekend. Thanks to the efforts of John Beck, Eric Bone, Elis Eberlein, Rick Hood, Terradan Landchild, Bruce McAlister and others, we will have a similar level of state wide activity in 1996. But wouldn't it be fun to have an event nearly every weekend in the greater Seattle area? I think so, and I think we can do it.
How would we staff these events? COC has about 150-170 members, and it takes a staff of about six to eight to put on an event (except Class A meets). If every club member helped at one (1) event each year, then 20+ events would be a breeze to host. Think what this would mean: an event roughly every other weekend. Sounds nice to me.
Recently the COC board increased meet fees with the goal of raising funds for map making. But that's the easy way out. It would be better, I believe, to work to increase meet attendance than to merely raise the fees. Increased attendance in many ways can be a permanent gain, while higher meet fees at best do nothing more than earn more money.

For COC: Produce or update at least one large area map each year, and at least two smaller maps.
Maps are the lifeblood of any orienteering club. As our mapping program increases, so too does the level of excitement. We come pretty close to this goal now; I would like to see it become an established practice.

Page 7

For COC: Produce a membership handbook and distribute it (free, as a membership benefit) to all club members.
This would contain the club charter, bylaws, and history; directions to and descriptions of club maps; job descriptions for meet staff; simple rules for orienteering meets ("always report to the finish" and "stay out of prohibited areas"); maybe even some basic hints on orienteering technique; a list of meet procedures; and summaries of club policies. This could be supplemented with an annual booklet giving club members' names and phone numbers (to facilitate such things as car pooling), current club officers, and the year's (tentative) schedule.

For all clubs: Make our events more inviting to first timers.
If a first timer comes to an event and has a bad experience—poor (frustrating) map, misplaced markers, too tough a course, indifferent meet staff, inadequate instruction, whatever—then there is a good chance that they will never come back (yes, this has happened). It is such an effort to get first timers to come to an event in the first place, it is the height of foolishness to fail to work to make their experience a positive one. So: better training of meet staff (which implies standardized meet procedures), a "greeter" at every event, and a general attitude of "let's put on a first class show", even (especially!) at small local events.

For everyone: Make our collective involvement in orienteering fun.
That's why we do this, isn't it?

Membership Report

Aaron the Alchemist

It is that time of year again, when most thoughts are towards Christmas and family. The same is true for the membership coordinator. However it is also the time when COC must make an official counting of its membership for USOF. As of 12/15/1995 COC boasted a cool 110 memberships total. Of these memberships, 59 were for individual members and 51 were for family/groups memberships. Under the USOF rules this give the club an official membership of 161. We added 32 new memberships this year (14 family and 18 individual). Actual membership is probably greater then the numbers above indicate. There are many renewal notices out that had not been returned by the 15th of December.

Field Notes

* John Rogers and Knut Olson proved their orienteering mettle beyond any doubt (although few of us doubted) by becoming U.S. Orienteering Champions in their age groups (M40 and M65) at the championships in Pontiac. Debbie Newell and Ken Lew also medaled, both taking third place finishes. (Results of all local competitors are in the results section.)

* Experienced orienteering cartographers are invited to CROC's first Mapping Seminar to share their wealth of experience! We are meeting at 10am on 27 January at Matt & Crystal's to discuss IOF standards, fieldworking techniques and possibly OCAD. We then will go over to Powell Butte and map a portion as a group to develop consistency and field mapping experience. We then plan to split up and map various sections of the park. There is no charge and CROC will supply the maps. Since CROC doesn't have an IOF mapper (yet) this is basically a "bootstrap" operation to pool our knowledge and develop our skills! If you'd like to join us, please call Matt & Crystal at (503)667-2367 for directions or e-mail: terradan@pacifier.com

I saw Jan Vrana in September (I missed the last Bearing due to another trip—sorry). We spent a short 2 hours in a bar in the Boston airport talking about all the orienteers we both know. I almost missed my flight to Halifax. Jan is working for the "rich and famous" (his words not mine) doing tile and art work in Aspen, Colorado. He left Honolulu two years ago after he and his first wife separated. He and his new girl friend were on their way to Slovakia for his father's 70th birthday party. He looked good but hasn't been orienteering too much. He sends greeting to all his orienteering friends in Seattle. —Anne York*

Most everyone in the club has now been contacted by the volunteer coordinator either through phone messages or voice to voice about assisting in meet registration and timing. I want to use this forum to thank all of those who have volunteered to date. And you may be glad to know, you will go to the bottom of my list. To those who haven't been able, I'll be giving you another chance. If you just didn't feel qualified enough, have no fear, there are no prerequisites to the registration or timing jobs. You may even substitute a willing spouse, significant other or teenager (15 or over) in your place. —Ron Brown*

* Bill Cusworth, who has done local regional rankings in the past (and now does the USOF rankings) said he is again willing to do local regional rankings for "B" and "A" meets, if there is interest. Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Inland Northwest Orienteering Cup

September 16-17, 1995
Riverside State Park, Spokane, WA
Eastern Washington Orienteering Club and Sacajawea Orienteers

Returning from the World Orienteering Championships in Germany in August, Eric Bone and Debbie Newell won the Inland Northwest Orienteering Cup trophies. The women's competition was a tight race; Eileen Breseman came within 21 seconds of Debbie's 103:27 time for the two-day competition. The event had an international scope with Stephen Mota from British Columbia winning the M-12 category and Sture Carlsson from Sweden winning M55-. With a 94:10 time on Saturday's 8km Course 4, John Rogers would have dominated the M35- category if he had not lost his control card somewhere between controls 9 and 10.

Elis Eberlein designed the courses, with courses 4 and 5 being "classic" (8 and 12 km, respectively) courses on Saturday and short "sprint" courses on Sunday. In addition to the distance, the 60 m climb to the final control tested participants' stamina, but everyone made it up the hill.

The twenty-nine people attending the Saturday dinner on the Gonzaga University campus catered by Marriott Food Services enjoyed a cake celebrating John Rogers' fortieth birthday, and many said the dinner compared favorably with past dinners at other locations.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

WIOL Season and Winter O' Series To Begin

Don't forget about the 1995-96 Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League season, which officially begins with the January 6 Ft. Steilacoom Park meet in Tacoma. Support the league by helping at a meet or bringing a student to a meet.

The first meet of the Winter O' Series will also be at Ft. Steilacoom. No pre-registration is required to participate in the Series. Just run the longest course at a minimum of 3 of the 5 designated Series meets. You will receive a score based on your placing at each meet you attend. Your best three scores will be totaled and male and female winners will be crowned at the final meet at Seward Park on February 24. The full Winter O' Series schedule follows:
January 6 – Ft. Steilacoom Park, Tacoma 14 – Marymoor Park, Redmond 27 – Carkeek Park, Seattle
February 10 – Bridle Trails Park, Kirkland 24 – Seward Park, Seattle
For more information about WIOL or the O' Series, contact Debbie Newell at 206-488-7471 or DebbieN@cuix.pscu.com.

COC's Life Service Award Recipients: An Interview
via electronic mail, by Rick Hood

This year's COC Life Service Award Recipients collectively have over 30 years of volunteer service in support of our sport. Accordingly, we thought you might like to hear from honorees Debbie Newell and Harvey Friedman in their own words. (The COC board also chose to recognize Mike Schuh for his 15 plus years of service with this award, but we just learned he declined. However, in the spirit of community and growing the sport strong, we have included his responses as well.) — Rick
Congratulations on your COC life service award recognition.
[Debbie] Thank you. It's always nice to be appreciated, even when you do something of your own choice which you enjoy.

[Harvey] Thank you. I am grateful for the honor. Might I suggest renaming it the 15 Year Service Award and try to see who qualifies for 5-year service awards at each annual meeting banquet/party. It might keep more members who can not 'win' medals in the woods interested longer. Many folks have dropped out of O' locally and not just those who have moved out of the area or who have had children. Anything to try to keep more people involved should be tried.
[Mike] As I describe elsewhere, I have declined the award.
Which of your many contributions over the years to COC are you most proud of and why?
[Debbie] My stint as newsletter editor was the most enjoyable, but also the most work. It's a key position in the club. The newsletter lets all our members know the club is alive and kicking.
[Harvey] I got Cascade O. C. 501(c)(3) status with the IRS in 1983-84, was able to maintain a viable club as president during the difficult period when

two new offshoot clubs formed, was event organizer/meet director for the 1990 PNWOF World Cup event which until 1993 WOC was the meet with the largest individual entrant attendance in the USA, was newsletter editor for 2 years, was membership coordinator for 8 years. Surprisingly enough I am still willing to do occasional course design and more often to work the registration table at local meets.

[Mike] Tough question. What am I most proud of? The fact that I have been a member of COC longer than just about anyone? That I probably have contributed to more maps, more events, and the development of more clubs than any other member? That I have introduced more people to orienteering than anyone else in the region? I don't think I can pick any specific contributions—in all immodesty, there are just too many to choose from. I think the activities that have made me feel the best about my involvement in orienteering are those where I have made someone else happy by introducing them to this wonderful sport. To toil long and hard creating a map (often in the rain), then set a course (also in the rain), and then help some first timers find their way around the course, and to have them come back to the finish, all excited and eager to try another course—this is much, much more rewarding to me than any "prestigious" award, and in fact such awards pale in comparison.
What path would you suggest to those who want to get more involved with helping COC evolve, and what direction do you think the club might wish to aim toward?

[Debbie] I think our club must aim toward getting more kids involved in the sport. We must also educate the general public about orienteering so they will realize that it is not destructive to the environment. All it takes to get involved with the club is a call to a board member or any other active member of the club.

[Harvey] To quote the Nike ad, 'just do it'. If one hasn't helped out before, is at a meet and thinks that now would be a good time to start, it is easy. After you finish your course(s), ask the meet director if you are needed to help pick up the markers. This is a good way to see where placement is on a course you might not have tried and will be welcomed by the tired course setter and meet director, both of whom had to rise early to provide such a successful event. With enough people helping, everyone is back in enough time to all go get refreshments together at a local (somewhat close) restaurant. If you ask, you will probably learn what is involved in putting on a local meet.

So, volunteer to be a timer at the next meet. The meet director will try to have enough people so that there can be early and late shifts so that every one gets a chance to do a course. Over 4 meets, do start timing, finish timing, results posting, registration. Then you will know how to handle the administrative side of a local meet and you will be ready to be a meet director yourself. By working closely with the course designer, you will learn something about courses and, after taking a class in course design, you will be ready to do your own courses in a local park with the help of a course consultant.

Thus, when there is stretch of 2 or 3 weekends when the usual club organizers all travel to out-of-state meets and you think there should be a local meet during that time, you will be experienced enough to organize and run a successful local meet for those not traveling and any newcomers you might entice. Such satisfaction.

[Mike] The first part of the question has an easy answer: attend the club's board meetings, as that is where decisions are made regarding the present and future of the club. For those members unable to attend a meeting, offer to help out at an event, either by calling the Meet Director ahead of time (preferred!), or by volunteering at the event to help pick up markers or clean up.

The second part has two answers: First, I really have no idea where the club "might wish to aim towards". Part of my criticisms of last Spring (which led to my resigning from the board) were on this precise point: the club has no stated set of goals. The second answer would be my thoughts on where I would prefer the club to head. As this is a long answer, I have written a separate article elsewhere in this newsletter.

In spite of, or maybe because of, your years of service, I know you still enjoy being out on a course. What makes orienteering enjoyable for you both locally and elsewhere, and what would you suggest in helping others discover and experience the same?

[Debbie] I enjoy orienteering because every meet is a different experience, with new challenges to meet. And I like traveling to other areas, meeting new people and seeing old friends. Orienteers are generally a wonderful group of people, no matter where you are in the world.

[Harvey] Because I still make so many errors due to lapses in concentration, I still am in search of the perfect run. I don't expect that will happen but nevertheless I get a feeling of satisfaction every time I choose a good route and the control marker is right there. I have a feeling of relief when I finally find it after whatever errors I might have made. I
try to design courses so that people will enjoy the sport as much as I do.

One gets both physical and mental exercise and gets to meet many interesting people. Being open to hosting out-of-area orienteers in your house or apartment lets you learn from others about places you might not have been before. When you can afford to, you can then travel to other areas and have reciprocal lodging provided as well. Important for me is that most orienteers do not smoke.

Of course, if you go to a small town in a foreign country where the non-orienteering locals don't travel much, you may be up talking way past mid-nite even though you have an early start the next day. Americans (not the obnoxious youngsters nor the groups of retirees) are objects of interest to many in the civilized world. It is much fun.

[Mike] Orienteering combines an intellectual challenge—problem solving—with (usually) outdoor exercise; since I enjoy both endeavors, I enjoy a sport that has both. Because orienteers do not physically compete against each other (as in boxing), the atmosphere around even the most competitive of events is a friendly one. My involvement in orienteering has led me to travel to places that I might never have visited otherwise, and as a consequence I have met a lot of interesting friends and have orienteered in a lot of different terrain (10 foreign countries, and more than half of the states).

I would encourage others to invite their friends and family to our local events, and to walk around a couple of courses with them. An individual's first experience with orienteering can determine whether they return for another event; it is very important, therefore, that everyone associated with an event do their very best to make that first experience an enjoyable one—at every event. The map should be accurate and legible, and the courses should be appropriate with the markers correctly placed. Meet registration should be simple and smooth, and results processing should be prompt and correct. In general, be friendly, be helpful, be enthusiastic, and, most of all, look like you're having fun.

[Debbie] Thank you again for the award. Orienteering is great sport because you can participate in so many different ways. You can be competitive or you can simply enjoy being out in the woods. You can become involved in club administration or you can be out in the field setting courses and mapping. No matter which way you go you can't make a bad route choice.

[Harvey] Orienteering is the greatest sport in the world. Anyone can participate as long as they can walk, see, and think. My personal long-term goal is to compete in the Swedish five-day in the men's 100+ year old class. There currently is a 90+ class with both men and women actively out in the woods.

Orienteering is a great family sport. All can do a course together to start and as the relative abilities separate people, they can do courses separately. Children who go out alone learn responsible decision making. There is no intimidation factor as in such sports as football and basketball that might discourage kids from playing. One of the reasons for the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (WIOL) is to help kids develop their self-confidence (not just to get more Cascade members, though that is nice, too). Invite a friend to try our fabulous sport.

[Mike] I feel it is unfortunate that COC's leadership has chosen to hand out "Life Service Awards" in this fashion. It appears to me that the award was created as a direct consequence of my resignation from the COC Executive Board last Spring, and this makes the award feel a bit artificial to me. Instead of acting on some of my grievances, the board decided to give me an award. Also, last year a "President's Service Award" was presented to Peter Golde. Why was there no such award this year? There does not seem to an established recognition and award program within the club.

My contributions to local orienteering were never designed to win me any award. If they had any coherent design (doubtful in some cases), it was to further the sport, to get more people involved in it. My rewards have always come from the joy I bring to the participants; a "prestigious" Life Service Award does little to improve upon this, and in fact seems superfluous.

Accepting the award will not improve orienteering in the region, nor will it introduce more people to the sport, nor will it make any new maps, and it certainly won't set any courses. Only action does these things. Instead of acting, however, the board invented an award. I do not wish to receive the award, and I feel that being forced to make this decision has in turn caused some ill will: for this I am sorry. I hope that we can put all of this fluff behind us and move forward. Thank you.
Again, on behalf of the club and the greater orienteering community, thank you all for your comments and congratulations Debbie and Harvey on your award; we are very proud of you.

SACO had its annual meeting on December 2. It had good showing of local participant and was followed by the traditional Swedish Christmas Smorgasbord. At the meeting the following Board members were chosen for one year terms: Elis Eberlein, President; Frank Skorina, Ruth Miller, Michel Billaux, and Bob Gruel.

The new thing for 1996 is that the meet program will contain several meet on Sunday afternoons rather than Saturday mornings. This will be tested to decrease the conflicts with other youth sports activities. SACO will also organize a two-day event in Walla Walla and Horn Rapids in April. This is event arranged to make it more feasible for travelers from other areas to visit our area and try our maps. This will be arranged on April 27-28.

Another discussion at the annual meeting was regarding organizing a major meet in our part of the state in cooperation with EWOC in Spokane. The discussion is to try to organize the Intercollegiate Championships 1997 which are traditionally held during the month of April. A possible area for such an event would be the area just south of Potholes Reservoir, south of Moses Lake. A decision about this has to be made in early 1996 to make the mapping project and organization possible. All possible help and input will be appreciated. I will probably write about this further in the next issue of Bearing.
There is a planned Ski-Orienteering event this winter at Lake Wenatchee on Sunday, March 3.

Ski Orienteering is a mixture of foot orienteering and cross country skiing. Taking place largely on groomed trails, ski orienteering challenges map reading, route finding and skiing techniques. For various reasons, ski- orienteering has been sort of left behind in the Northwest, so set aside March 3 to experience the challenge and fun of a day in the snow.

Clinic For those who want or need a little guidance or refresher in cross country technique, there will be a clinic Saturday, March 2 at Bruce McAlister's cabin near Scenic. The clinic will be from 10 to 2. Following the clinic, some hardy souls will ski or snowshoe into the Scenic Hot Springs. Those who will be continuing on to Lake Wenatchee are welcome to stay overnight at the cabin. It is about a 1 k walk or ski in to the rustic cabin: no electricity, no running water, but lots of atmosphere. Bring a sleeping bag. Dinner and breakfast is $10.
Ski-O Meet The meet will be on groomed trails at Lake Wenatchee State Park. There will be a B/W map. Starts are from 10 to 12 at the skiing parking lot (area will be signed).

Directions For the clinic proceed over the Stevens Pass Highway (US 2) to the chain-up area at Scenic, about 2 miles beyond Deception Falls. Proceed to the far end of the chain-up area and park in plowed area with cabins in the background. This is NOT a Sno-Park area. Take the old Stevens Pass road across from parking area about 1 k to cabin (signed). Approximately 60 miles from Seattle.

For the meet continue about 5 miles on US 2 to Stevens Pass and continue about 19 miles to the Lake Wenatchee turn-off. The turn-off from US 2 is well signed. The park itself is about 3 miles north of US 2, and is well marked. Proceed to the cross country ski-area parking lot. The start area will be signed from here. This parking area IS a sno-park area, and your vehicle must have a permit.

Costs Meet Costs: $5/person; $2 for juniors under 18. (Dinner and breakfast optional Saturday night - $10). Please note this meet is sponsored by the the US Ski Orienteering Team, and is not sponsored by any local club. There are NO club or USOF discounts. All proceeds over expenses go to the US Ski Orienteering Team.

A Vision for Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest

Last Spring I asked the Executive Board of Cascade OC "what are the club's goals? where are we headed?" Not too surprisingly, no one had an answer—COC had no stated goals, nor any kind of long term plan. We seem to proceed in fits and starts from one event to the next, from one year to another. Often, club events come upon us before we are ready for them, and what little advance planning that is done takes on the air of last minute crisis management. I don't think it has to be this way.

I would like to propose a set of long term goals for orienteering in the Pacific Northwest. Some of these might seem outlandish, or even impossible, while others would appear to be obvious. In the absence of any kind of long range discussion elsewhere, let me present a few possible goals, and then suggest how we might reach them. For each goal, I ask that we first agree that it is a worthy goal, and then figure how we can make it happen. Too often these discussions run into attitudes like "we can't do that" (which almost guarantees that we can't!) and "who'd want to do that?" Please, let's separate the "what" (the goal) from the "how". And remember that these are long term goals...

Develop more small clubs, and help existing clubs survive and grow. Specifically, develop clubs in the following Washington cities: Bremerton/Poulsbo, Port Townsend, Port Angeles, Hoquiam/Aberdeen, Centralia/Chehalis, Yakima, Wenatchee, Omak/Okanogan, Moses Lake, and Pullman/Moscow (ID). In Oregon: Eugene, Albany/Corvalis, Salem, Bend/Redmond, The Dalles, Astoria, Roseburg, Klamath Falls, Pendleton, and Baker. Idaho: Boise, Pocatello, Idaho Falls, Twin Falls, Sandpoint, and Coeur d' Alene. Montana is tougher, but start with the bigger cities: Missoula, Butte, Helena, Great Falls, and Billings. Some of these could be combined to start with - Sandpoint and Coeur d' Alene will probably operate as a "chapter" of the EWOC in the short term, and initially there would be just one club on the West side of Puget Sound.

I firmly believe that a rising tide of orienteering will lift all clubs. That is, having a club just down the road from another club will help both clubs grow and prosper. Here in Seattle we see this symbiosis with the high degree of cooperation between COC, SAMM, and HUSKY. In Eastern Washington, despite the distance involved, EWOC and SACO are working together on various events and projects. As we form more clubs, this just gets better. If we had nearly twenty clubs in Washington State, then the attendance at major events would increase and hosting them would be less of a financial risk. In addition, the registration fees from such a large orienteering population would provide the funds necessary to produce more maps. In addition, I feel that a larger orienteering populace will help us lobby for land access.

In other words, the promotion and growth of orienteering are much more important than "prestigious" awards, ranking systems, making O' an Olympic sport, earning a World Championships title, or just about anything else. If the sport grows, then these other goals become easier; conversely, these other goals do little by themselves to expand orienteering.
And, besides, it'll just be a lot more fun.

We also need to take care of what we have. Several clubs are just barely hanging on, in particular JSOC in Medford and CHUKO in Anacortes. We need to help these clubs become self-sufficient and stable.

Host at least six two day Class A meets each year. Might as well suggest an "outlandish" one. The presumption here is that Class A meets are worth hosting and attending. For those who care about national ranking points, this is arguably the best way to earn them. For the rest of us, a Class A meet means (or is supposed to mean) top notch organization with good courses on a first rate map. I feel they are worth doing, and I think that we could host more than we presently do. COC could host one a year (we even agreed to do this a year or two ago, but...) while SAMM, NISQ, CROC, and EWOC/SACO/EOC could host one every other year. This would give us three such events each year. As other clubs develop and grow, they could host additional events (this is why goal #1 is #1).
Sure this is a long term goal, but think what it means: instead of using up vacation time and spending big bucks to fly all around the country, we could DRIVE to Class A meets. Wouldn't that be nice?
Make at least one of these Class A meets an IOF event. Why not? Mostly it's a bit of extra paper work, but it would give us some wider exposure, and perhaps a larger attendance.
Host at least one national championship each year. Similar to #3. COC has discussed hosting the Intercollegiate Championships as well as the US
Championships; we hosted the Short Course Championships in 1994.

Expand WIOL beyond the greater Seattle area. Well, first we need to focus on increasing the level of participation within the present WIOL area, but expansion throughout Western Washington should be feasible and rewarding. During the 1994-95 season, the league essentially was a one-school league (Bremerton HS students were about 70-80% of the attendance at most of the meets). The long term health of the league depends on expanding it to other schools. Climatic concerns make expansion in Eastern Washington a bit problematic - the present Winter season won't work over there. Expansion anywhere, however, is dependent on clubs and maps existing in those new areas. Hence goal #1. We could also explore adoption of orienteering by WIAA (the state high school athletics association). At the moment, I think WIOL is too small, but we could investigate what it requires and what it would mean. The benefit is that schools could issue varsity letters to participants, which would mean more exposure for the sport.

Host the USOF convention at least once every five to ten years. Our best chance to influence national O' policy, and to gain the right to host national events. Plus, it's a money maker and brings national expertise to our area.

Have a Class A map within an hour's drive of 90% of Washington's population by 1998. Actually, we're almost there now. Trollhaugen, Drunken Charlie Lake, and Tenalquot are each within an hour's drive of most of the Seattle/Tacoma/Olympia area (75% of the state's population), and Riverside sits on the edge of Spokane. If we upgrade and expand Fire Mtn. or make a map near North Bend (or Bremerton), and CROC maps Camp Bonneville, then we'd probably have it. But why bother with such a goal? Because this would mean that orienteering is accessible to just about everyone in the state. The farther away the event (regardless of its nature), the greater the resistance for first timers to go and try it. This goal could be reworded as "have an orienteering map (of any size) within a 30 minute drive of 100% of Washington's population by 2000". This might be more useful. It will also be more work... (45 minutes, 98%, and 2005 might be more realistic - but the point is to have a goal).
For WSOA: Publish a guide book to regional orienteering maps. Something simple like how to get to each map in the region. Nothing fancy, and it could be a fund raiser.

For COC: Host at least 24 days of orienteering each year, and work to increase the attendance at each event. Events are orienteering clubs' raison d'etre—they are how we teach the sport, and they are our largest source of income. Yes, they take work to organize and host, but it is foolish to consciously hold fewer of them, as is often proposed. Yes, there are other clubs in the immediate area that host events, but COC usually does not earn any income from those events. I strongly believe that both the club and the sport benefit from having an active schedule. If, at some future date, our events average 200 or more in attendance, then we won't need as many of them to generate revenue. COC could then reduce our event schedule, but this would make sense only when there are other clubs able to fill the void (see goal #1!). In 1995, there was an orienteering event somewhere in Washington State nearly every weekend. Thanks to the efforts of John Beck, Eric Bone, Elis Eberlein, Rick Hood, Terradan Landchild, Bruce McAlister and others, we will have a similar level of state wide activity in 1996. But wouldn't it be fun to have an event nearly every weekend in the greater Seattle area? I think so, and I think we can do it. How would we staff these events? COC has about 150-170 members, and it takes a staff of about six to eight to put on an event (except Class A meets). If every club member helped at one (1) event each year, then 20+ events would be a breeze to host. Think what this would mean: an event roughly every other weekend. Sounds nice to me. Recently the COC board increased meet fees with the goal of raising funds for map making. But that's the easy way out. It would be better, I believe, to work to increase meet attendance than to merely raise the fees. Increased attendance in many ways can be a permanent gain, while higher meet fees at best do nothing more than earn more money.

For COC: Produce or update at least one large area map each year, and at least two smaller maps. Maps are the lifeblood of any orienteering club. As our mapping program increases, so too does the level of excitement. We come pretty close to this goal now; I would like to see it become an established practice.

For COC: Produce a membership handbook and distribute it (free, as a membership benefit) to all club members. This would contain the club charter, bylaws, and history; directions to and descriptions of club maps; job descriptions for meet staff; simple rules for orienteering meets ("always report to the finish" and "stay out of prohibited areas"); maybe even some basic hints on orienteering technique; a list of meet procedures; and summaries of club policies. This could be supplemented with an annual booklet giving club members' names and phone numbers (to facilitate such things as car pooling), current club officers, and the year's (tentative) schedule.

For all clubs: Make our events more inviting to first timers. If a first timer comes to an event and has a bad experience—poor (frustrating) map, misplaced markers, too tough a course, indifferent meet staff, inadequate instruction, whatever—then there is a good chance that they will never come back (yes, this has happened). It is such an effort to get first timers to come to an event in the first place, it is the height of foolishness to fail to work to make their experience a positive one. So: better training of meet staff (which implies standardized meet procedures), a "greeter" at every event, and a general attitude of "let's put on a first class show", even (especially!) at small local events.
For everyone: Make our collective involvement in orienteering fun. That's why we do this, isn't it?

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Returning from the World Orienteering Championships in Germany in August, Eric Bone and Debbie Newell won the Inland Northwest Orienteering Cup trophies. The women's competition was a tight race; Eileen Breseman came within 21 seconds of Debbie's 103:27 time for the two-day competition. The event had an international scope with Stephen Mota from British Columbia winning the M-12 category and Sture Carlsson from Sweden winning M55-. With a 94:10 time on Saturday's 8km Course 4, John Rogers would have dominated the M35- category if he had not lost his control card somewhere between controls 9 and 10.

Elis Eberlein designed the courses, with courses 4 and 5 being "classic" (8 and 12 km, respectively) courses on Saturday and short "sprint" courses on Sunday. In addition to the distance, the 60 m climb to the final control tested participants' stamina, but everyone made it up the hill.

The twenty-nine people attending the Saturday dinner on the Gonzaga University campus catered by Marriott Food Services enjoyed a cake celebrating John Rogers' fortieth birthday, and many said the dinner compared favorably with past dinners at other locations.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
